hypothesis: A LiteDRAM A7DDRPHY no-ODELAY variant can avoid the YPCB HR-bank output-ODELAY P&R blocker and produce an open bitstream for autonomous DDR3 init/bandwidth probing.

v26_seed2_native_flow.interpretation: JEDEC init and native scheduler completed; data integrity failed.

v30_native_read_sampler.interpretation: The native command path completes, the lower 32 bits are stuck at zero across sampled final reads, and the upper 32 bits vary by address.

v31_dfii_direct_round_trip.interpretation: LiteDRAM/LiteX JEDEC init, DFII CSR commands, and JTAG readback are alive on board; direct DFII data still mismatches without read-leveling.

v32_litex_read_leveling_scan.interpretation: The LiteX-style per-module read-leveling scan completes all 2048 candidates, but no byte lane has a zero-error window; this shifts the next hypothesis away from read delay/bitslip alone and toward DFII packing, write phase, byte-lane direction, or command/data alignment.

v34_big_csr_order_fix.interpretation: Reversing DFII wrdata/rddata subregister order to match CONFIG_CSR_ORDERING_BIG changed the failure from scrambled data to structured byte-ramp mismatch, proving the CSR-order bug was real but not the only issue.

v35_write_bitslip_scan.interpretation: Adding write-bitslip to the scan showed write-side alignment matters: modules 4 and 6 reached zero-error candidates. The final captured DFII words were still the last candidate rather than a final all-selected verification pass, so v35 did not prove a selected-lane pass.

v36_50mhz_final_selected_probe.interpretation: Regenerating LiteDRAM at 50 MHz made the generated clocking and P&R target agree, but final all-selected byte-ramp verification still failed. The first two 32-bit DFII words matched exactly and later words repeated earlier values, pointing at burst/phase/write-data sequencing rather than gross timing.

v37_dfii_pattern_classifier.interpretation: A uniform 16-word DFII burst round-trips with zero mismatches, while phase-constant and byte-ramp patterns fail. This strongly narrows the remaining bug to DFI burst/phase/write-data sequencing or data-order assumptions; it is no longer a generic no-ODELAY PHY, pin-map, CSR, JTAG, or gross timing failure.

v38_command_phase_sweep.interpretation: Changing DFII write/read command phase alone did not produce a variable-pattern pass.

v39_wrdata_commit_order.interpretation: Changing the WRDATA CSR commit order did not fix the structured DFII variable-pattern mismatch.

v40_direct_native_shortcut.interpretation: Skipping the proven DFII/calibration setup regressed to an init/probe error; this is not DDR3 data-integrity evidence.

v41_restored_setup_native_bist.readback_signature: sampled readback varies almost entirely in logical byte lane 4 ([39:32])
v41_restored_setup_native_bist.interpretation: Restoring the proven setup brought native command flow back; the remaining failure is a data-lane collapse, not init or scheduler deadlock.

v42_quick_lane_setting.correction: Later code inspection showed this image skipped only lane 0 and still scanned lanes 1-7; treat it as an intermediate diagnostic, not a global-lane-setting proof.

v43_byte_enable_diagnostic.byte_enable_result: Only logical byte lane 4 survived cleanly in this biased image.
v43_byte_enable_diagnostic.interpretation: The byte-enable diagnostic matched the native full-word readback signature, but the lane-0 shortcut meant a clean full-scan rerun was needed.

v44_clean_full_scan_byte_enable.interpretation: Clean full-scan evidence confirms that variable DFII/native data and masked byte writes collapse through logical byte lane 4. The next target is byte/phase association, not another broad delay sweep.

v54_clean_dfii_jtag_payload.interpretation: The v53 overlapping-payload artifact was removed. Clean v54 evidence shows init and command plumbing complete, but native and DFII data paths still fail.

v55_lowrate_probe.interpretation: Dropping the no-ODELAY LiteDRAM PHY from sys=50 MHz/sys4x=200 MHz to sys=25 MHz/sys4x=100 MHz does not recover DDR3 data. This weakens the marginal high-rate timing hypothesis and points the next probe at PHY/DFI command, address, byte/phase association, DQ/DQS grouping, or board control wiring.

dq_dqs_lane_report.interpretation: The UCF lane groups match the basic LiteDRAM expectation that dq[lane*8..lane*8+7] share the same package bank as dqs[lane]. This does not prove bit order or DFI phase/beat order, but makes a crude cross-bank DQ/DQS grouping mistake unlikely.

v56_dfii_column_address_association_prob.interpretation: The low-rate no-ODELAY design can return stable nonzero direct DFII data, but the data does not match any write/read command phase or any tested address-tagged column slot. This weakens a simple column-address-offset hypothesis and points the next loop at DQS phase/latency and DFI write/read phase/beat association.

v56_dqs0_lowrate_dqs_phase_discriminator.interpretation: Changing only the routed sys4x_dqs phase from 90 degrees to 0 degrees leaves the board/JTAG signature unchanged. The artifact is a real phase variant, so this weakens the hypothesis that the present blocker is simply the no-ODELAY DQS phase choice.

v57_dfii_no_write_discriminator.purpose: Suppress only the direct-DFII write command to test whether the v56 DFII readback is stale/write-independent or influenced by issuing writes.

guards.old_odelay_negative_controls: EXPECTED_FAIL: HR-bank IOB33/IOB33M outputs do not have ODELAY

v57_dfii_no_write_discriminator.interpretation: Suppressing the DFII write command changes the direct-DFII board/JTAG signature relative to v56/v56-dqs0, so the v56 readback was not purely stale and write-independent. This does not prove DRAM write acceptance; the next target is DFI write-data/read-data phase or beat association, byte-mask/DM semantics, per-bit order, or read capture latency.

v58_dfii_phase_source_matrix.purpose: Run a compact single-bitstream matrix that writes phase-tagged data on one selected DFII source phase at a time and sweeps read command phase through the existing JTAG payload.

implementation.matrix_encoding: index[3:2] selects write-data source phase and write command phase; index[1:0] selects read command phase

v58_dfii_phase_source_matrix.interpretation: No matrix entry matched the expected phase-tagged data. The result is not the v57 no-write edge-byte artifact: with writes enabled, source/write phase 0 produces nonzero readback for all read phases while source/write phases 1..3 read back zero. This narrows the next split toward DFI phase association or write-phase gating, but still does not prove DRAM write acceptance.
v58_dfii_phase_source_matrix.next_gate: Decouple write-data source phase from write command phase, then add a DM/mask polarity discriminator before rowstream or TinyStories integration.

no_odelay_obuf_cutout.pin_topology: data_i -> OBUF -> data_o on the same DDR3-relevant HR-bank cutout pin set

no_odelay_obufds_cutout.pin_topology: data_i -> OBUFDS -> data_p/data_n on the same DDR3-relevant HR-bank cutout pin set

odelay_obuf_cutout.error: BEL IOB_X0Y92/IOB33/OUTBUF is located on a high range bank. High range banks do not have ODELAY

odelay_obufds_cutout.error: BEL IOB_X0Y94/IOB33M/OUTBUF is located on a high range bank. High range banks do not have ODELAY

open_flow_regression_guards.interpretation: The old K7DDRPHY ODELAY topology remains the rejected topology. Plain HR-bank output buffers route cleanly, and the generated no-ODELAY LiteDRAM RTL has the required phase-shifted sys4x_dqs clock with zero ODELAYE2 instances.

current_interpretation: The open LiteDRAM/LiteX flow is not the blocker: synthesis, P&R, programming, JEDEC init, DFII CSR commands, and JTAG readback work.
The no-ODELAY regression guards pass: generated LiteDRAM RTL has no ODELAYE2, and minimal HR-bank OBUF/OBUFDS cutouts route cleanly.
The remaining blocker is DDR3 data integrity.
The byte-map diagnostic does not show a clean physical-module to native-byte permutation.
The v30 sampler shows the lower 32 bits stuck at zero across the first eight final native readback samples.
The v31 DFII direct round-trip returns nonzero data but mismatches all 16 words.
The v32 LiteX-style read-leveling scan completes all candidates but finds no zero-error window, so read delay/bitslip alone is not sufficient.
The v34 CSR-order fix, v35 write-bitslip scan, and v36 final-selected pass narrowed the failure to structured DFII burst data repetition.
The v37 classifier proves a uniform DFII burst passes while phase-constant and byte-ramp bursts fail, so the next target is DFI burst/phase/write-data sequencing.
The v38 command-phase sweep found no passing write/read phase combination.
The v41-v44 native and byte-enable probes show variable data collapsing through logical byte lane 4 even after a clean full per-lane scan.
The v42 quick lane-setting image was not a true global-lane-setting proof and should not be cited as one.
The v54 clean payload confirms the integrated native and DFII failures without the v53 decoder overlap artifact.
The v55 low-rate probe shows that sys=25 MHz/sys4x=100 MHz still returns all-zero native and byte-enable data, so the blocker is unlikely to be only marginal high-rate timing.
The DQ/DQS lane report shows all nine UCF byte-lane groups are package-bank consistent with their matching DQS pins, making a crude cross-bank lane-grouping error less likely.
The v56 DFII column-address association probe returns stable nonzero direct DFII data at every tested column but no matches, weakening a simple column-address-offset hypothesis.
The v56-dqs0 phase discriminator changes the routed sys4x_dqs phase to 0 degrees but produces the same raw board/JTAG signature, weakening a simple DQS-phase-only explanation.

The v57 no-write discriminator changes the raw direct-DFII signature when only the DFII write command is suppressed, so v56 was not purely stale/write-independent readback, but it does not prove DRAM write acceptance.
The v58 phase/source matrix finds no exact phase-tagged match; only source/write phase 0 produces nonzero readback for all read phases, while source/write phases 1..3 read back zero.

The current blocker is DFI phase association or write-phase gating, DM semantics, DQS/DQ beat order, per-bit order, or read capture behavior in the no-ODELAY LiteDRAM path, not the open implementation flow itself.
latest_litedram_litex_debug.next_gate: Decouple DFII write-data source phase from write command phase, then add a compact DM/mask polarity discriminator before returning to native-port bandwidth or rowstream integration.

what_was_learned: The open LiteDRAM/LiteX DDR3 lane can now synthesize, place, route, emit a bitstream, program the board, and expose an autonomous JTAG payload.
The K7DDRPHY failure was not a full-controller scale issue; the A7DDRPHY no-ODELAY variant avoids the HR-bank output-ODELAY blocker.
The no-ODELAY cutouts prove the same HR-bank output-buffer sites route when ODELAYE2 is removed.
JEDEC init, native writes, native reads, DFII CSR commands, and JTAG sampling all complete.

The remaining blocker is DDR3 data integrity: sampled native reads have the lower 32 bits stuck at zero, direct DFII readback mismatches, and the v32 read-leveling scan finds no zero-error lane window.
The v37 pattern classifier proves the physical path can round-trip a uniform DFII burst, but variable phase/beat data still aliases or repeats.

The v44 clean full-scan byte-enable diagnostic shows the variable-data failure collapses through logical byte lane 4; the next experiment should map byte/phase association rather than widen delay sweeps.
The v55 low-rate bitstream still fails after reducing the no-ODELAY PHY to sys=25 MHz/sys4x=100 MHz, so another broad clock-rate sweep is not the best next loop.
The DQ/DQS lane report makes a crude cross-bank byte-lane grouping mistake unlikely.
The v56 low-rate DFII address sweep returns nonmatching nonzero data for every tested column, so a simple column-address-offset explanation is also unlikely.
The v56-dqs0 routed phase variant returns the same board/JTAG signature as the 90-degree image, so a simple sys4x_dqs phase-only explanation is unlikely.

The v57 no-write discriminator changes the direct-DFII readback pattern when the DFII write command is suppressed, so the prior direct-DFII readback is influenced by issuing writes but is not yet proof of DRAM write acceptance.
The v58 phase/source matrix shows source/write phase 0 is the only row that produces nonzero readback, and none of the phase-tagged patterns match.
decision.next_gate: Decouple write-data source phase from write command phase and add a DM/mask polarity discriminator before returning to broader DQS/write-data/read-latency sweeps, native-port bandwidth, or rowstream integration.
